item.title: Is It Too Much to Ask to Care About Sudan?
item.description: I have seen what can happen when Americans care about a wrong in the world.

item.title: I Went to China to See Their Progress on A.I. We Can’t Beat Them.
item.description: American leaders should be trying to cooperate with China on A.I.

item.title: Jesus Is ‘a Light That’s Both Historical and Eternal’
item.description: The most monstrous pictures of God come from the Christian tradition just as the most radiant images of God come from what Christians believe.

item.title: The End of Pax Americana
item.description: America is still a global force, says the Opinion columnist Carlos Lozada. But Trump’s desire to “wield superpowers” without behaving like a superpower is putting its reputation as a reliable leader for peace at risk.

item.title: Israel’s War Strategy is to Avoid Peace
item.description: If war is the status quo, the job is never done, and you can never lose.

item.title: Orban’s Fall Is an Astounding Achievement
item.description: Voters seized the chance to leave behind his 16-year rule.

item.title: No, Trump Is Not a Genius Negotiator
item.description: Were President Trump’s recent posts about Iran a masterstroke of negotiation or the “willful, reckless destruction” of America’s reputation? The foreign policy analyst Fareed Zakaria tells the columnist Ezra Klein that the administration’s pursuit of spectacle has left the U.S. weaker and Iran more capable of holding the global economy hostage.

item.title: Trump, Unchecked and Unpredictable
item.description: Readers respond to a guest essay, “Trump Is Turning America Into a Psychotic State.” Also: Viktor Orban’s defeat in Hungary.

item.title: The Moral Cost of Trump’s War
item.description: The president’s recent posts about Iran may signal the end of America’s reputation as a country guided by morality, the columnist Ezra Klein argues. The foreign policy expert Fareed Zakaria joins Ezra to unpack how we got here, and where we’re headed next.

item.title: Pete Hegseth’s Gospel of Carnage
item.description: The defense secretary thinks he’s commanding God’s army

item.title: Who, Exactly, Still Likes Ye?
item.description: Ye’s rage against the machine has seemingly come to epitomize badass for many young people.

item.title: How Trump’s ‘Dealmakers in Peace’ Failed in Iran
item.description: Trump’s team learns peace can’t be brokered like a business deal.

item.title: The Truth About Mary Todd Lincoln
item.description: Retelling the story of a woman today requires a motivation to correct the historical record.

item.title: What Orban’s Defeat Means for the Rest of the World
item.description: The Hungarian prime minister’s defeat demonstrates that opposition to his party was able to overwhelm all the structures Orban put in place to protect his rule.

item.title: Oil Companies Want What Gun Manufacturers Have
item.description: The fossil fuel industry has mounted a carefully orchestrated campaign to stop climate lawsuits and saddle the public with the costs of climate change.

item.title: The Iran War Has Made Clear the Old Arrangements Are Over
item.description: For decades, the U.S.-Gulf relationship was based on oil for security. That framework is obsolete.

item.title: Iran Is Losing the Cyberwar, Not the Real War
item.description: Tehran’s efforts have been underwhelming, but its conventional military successes have been significant enough that it may not matter.

item.title: Hasan Piker Is Not the Enemy
item.description: Conversation is not a reward to be bestowed on those with whom we agree; it’s a base line practice.

item.title: An Iran Cease-Fire Won’t Help Gas Prices
item.description: Why a cease-fire in Iran won’t bring gasoline prices down.

item.title: When Should Judges Sound the Alarm?
item.description: A retired judge and a law professor respond to a Page A1 article. Also: Censoring college professors; executive power after President Trump.

item.title: Trump’s War With Iran Has Weakened America
item.description: The president should at long last recognize the ineptitude of his impulsive, go-it-alone approach.

item.title: A New Era of World War Has Arrived
item.description: As the conflicts in Ukraine and the Middle East drag on, the world war is returning.

item.title: Parents, Consider Underachieving
item.description: I am just so tired, and so bad at crafts.

item.title: Trump’s War of Choice Will Become a War of Regret
item.description: America may be losing more than just the conflict in Iran.

item.title: Trump’s Iran War Is a Familiar Middle Eastern Folly
item.description: American power can survive America’s recurring overreach.

item.title: The Sphinx Thinks It Stinks
item.description: Melania creates another mystery with a surprise statement.

item.title: He Was ‘Trump Before Trump.’ Now He’s in Trouble.
item.description: An Orban defeat would have serious implications for the conservative movement worldwide.

item.title: When Technology Outstrips Morality in War
item.description: Readers respond to a guest essay about the U.S. and Israeli missteps in the conflict in Iran.

item.title: Trump’s War of Choice Will Become a War of Regret
item.description: America may be losing more than just the conflict in Iran.

item.title: Europe Is Done With Appeasing Trump
item.description: Most Europeans, and much of the world, have concluded that no amount of flattery will win more than fleeting approval from President Trump.

item.title: Prices Are High. Here’s One Reason Not to Panic.
item.description: I believe the gas tank is half full.

item.title: A Republican Strategist Explains What’s Going On With the Party
item.description: Without Trump on the midterms ballot, how will his party fare?

item.title: How Ben Sasse Is Living Now That He Is Dying
item.description: The former senator wants to heal the America he’s leaving behind.

item.title: How Haiti Can Dismantle Its Gangs
item.description: Force alone can’t stabilize a country in chaos.

item.title: Fareed Zakaria on the Moral Cost of Trump’s War
item.description: The foreign policy analyst Fareed Zakaria explains how the Iran war has been a turning point in America’s standing in the world.

item.title: Fareed Zakaria on the Moral Cost of Trump’s War
item.description: The foreign policy analyst Fareed Zakaria explains how the Iran war has been a turning point in America’s standing in the world.

item.title: Gaza’s Rubble Is the Grave of Its Future
item.description: Six months after the cease-fire, ordinary Gazans contemplate their future while living with the wounds of war.

item.title: Vance Should Stand Up to Trump
item.description: Responses to Dana Milbank’s Opinion guest essay about Vice President JD Vance. Also: Melania Trump’s lament; a lost childhood; propaganda and facts.

item.title: Trump Is Turning America Into a Psychotic State
item.description: What are the implications if the government of the world’s most powerful country is chaotic in its thinking and not reliably in touch with reality?

item.title: For Putin, Iran Is Something Close to Irreplaceable
item.description: Vladimir Putin has spent years building a coalition of the discontented on the premise that authoritarian states can outlast Western pressure. Iran is his proof of concept.

item.title: Contemplating Trump at 80
item.description: The president is giving old people a bad name.

item.title: Artemis II Brings the Country a Much-Needed Sense of Wonder
item.description: Remembering the Apollo missions.

item.title: Who Was Most Weakened by This War? Trump? Iran? 3 Opinion Writers Debate.
item.description: Will the shooting really stop? What should be Trump’s red lines? A discussion on where America’s war on Iran stands.

item.title: Did Woke Go Too Far?
item.description: On “The Opinions,” Nadja Spiegelman explores why careful language is falling out of favor.

item.title: What Dying Feels Like
item.description: What does terminal cancer feel like? The former senator Ben Sasse describes the complicated “algorithm” of managing the pain that comes with his diagnosis on “Interesting Times.”

item.title: What A.I. and D.E.I. Have in Common
item.description: A cloud of doubt casts a wide shadow.

item.title: Trump Pardoned Them. Now They’re Committing Crimes Again.
item.description: When President Trump returned to office, he pardoned about 1,500 Jan. 6 offenders. Since then, many of them have been charged with new crimes, including assault and child abuse. As the midterm elections approach, the Times editorial board argues that the Republican Party should pay a political price for prioritizing loyalty over public safety.

item.title: Ditching Digital Junk Food for a Healthier Mind
item.description: Readers respond to an Opinion guest essay about digital consumption. Also: A failure of leadership; deconstructing Trump-speak; “moon joy.”
